content:
# James Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translations compared:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

## Summary
- **Total verses:** 17
- **Verses with full agreement:** 2 (vv. 7, 16 — near-identical across all three)
- **Verses with disagreements:** 15
- **Total disagreements logged:** 27
- **Most contested verse:** 4:5 (3 disagreements — textual variant, lexical, phrasing)

## Disagreement Categories
- **Lexical (word choice):** 10
- **Phrasing (sentence structure/word order):** 10
- **Addition (material not in Greek):** 4
- **Paraphrase (significant departure from source):** 1
- **Textual/Interpretive (manuscript variant):** 1
- **Capitalization (convention):** 1

---

## Full Disagreement Log

### 4:1 — Phrasing
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the pleasures of you, the ones warring in the members of you" |
| LOGOS | "your pleasures that wage war in your members" |
| PNEUMA | "the whole army of selfish desires that wage war within you" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"your pleasures that wage war in your members"** |
| Rationale | LOGOS balances fidelity and fluency. PNEUMA adds "army" and "selfish" — not in the Greek. |

### 4:2a — Phrasing
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "because of the not asking of you" |
| LOGOS | "because you do not ask" |
| PNEUMA | "because you don't ask God for it" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"because you do not ask"** |
| Rationale | EMET too wooden for English. PNEUMA adds "God" (not explicit in Greek). LOGOS is faithful and natural. |

### 4:2b — Addition (PNEUMA)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | (follows Greek closely) |
| LOGOS | (follows Greek closely) |
| PNEUMA | "so you scheme and even kill to get it... to take it from them" |
| **SOPHIA** | **No additions** |
| Rationale | PNEUMA adds interpretive expansions ("scheme," "to get it," "to take it from them") not found in the source text. |

### 4:3 — Lexical
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "badly you ask" |
| LOGOS | "you ask wrongly" |
| PNEUMA | "your motives are all wrong" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"you ask wrongly"** |
| Rationale | Greek kakos = wrongly/badly. LOGOS is the most accurate English equivalent. PNEUMA over-interprets. |

### 4:4a — Lexical (address)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "Adulteresses" |
| LOGOS | "Adulteresses" |
| PNEUMA | "You unfaithful people" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Adulteresses"** |
| Rationale | moichalides is feminine. EMET/LOGOS preserve the OT bridal-covenant metaphor. PNEUMA neutralizes the gendered metaphor. |

### 4:4b — Lexical (verb)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "might purpose" |
| LOGOS | "wills" |
| PNEUMA | "chooses" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"chooses"** |
| Rationale | Greek boulethen implies deliberate, volitional choice. "Chooses" captures this force better than "wills" or "might purpose." |

### 4:4c — Phrasing (result clause)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "an enemy of God is established" |
| LOGOS | "makes himself an enemy of God" |
| PNEUMA | "becomes God's enemy" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"makes himself an enemy of God"** |
| Rationale | kathistatai (middle/passive) implies a self-constituting act. LOGOS captures the reflexive sense well. |

### 4:4d — Addition (PNEUMA)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| PNEUMA | "I say it again" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Omitted** |
| Rationale | Not in the Greek text. PNEUMA's rhetorical addition for emphasis. |

### 4:5a — Lexical (adverb)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "emptily" |
| LOGOS | "to no purpose" |
| PNEUMA | "without meaning" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to no purpose"** |
| Rationale | Greek kenos = empty/vain. "To no purpose" best conveys the adverbial sense in idiomatic English. |

### 4:5b — Textual/Interpretive (verb form)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the Spirit which dwelt in us" (katokesen — intransitive aorist) |
| LOGOS | "the spirit which he caused to dwell in us" (katokisen — causative aorist) |
| PNEUMA | "the Spirit that God caused to dwell in us" (causative) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the Spirit that God caused to dwell in us"** |
| Rationale | LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on the causative reading. The manuscript is ambiguous (one letter difference), but the causative has broad support. EMET's intransitive reading is defensible but a minority position here. |

### 4:5c — Phrasing (verb phrase)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "long with envy" |
| LOGOS | "yearns jealously" |
| PNEUMA | "yearns jealously over us" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"yearns jealously"** |
| Rationale | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on "yearns jealously." PNEUMA adds "over us" (not explicit in Greek). EMET's "long with envy" is unclear in modern English. |

### 4:6 — Addition (PNEUMA)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| PNEUMA | "to meet that need" |
| **SOPHIA** | **Omitted** |
| Rationale | Interpretive gloss not present in the Greek text. |

### 4:7 — Phrasing (connective)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "Submit yourselves therefore" |
| LOGOS | "Submit yourselves therefore" |
| PNEUMA | "So then, submit yourselves" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Submit yourselves therefore"** |
| Rationale | EMET/LOGOS agree. PNEUMA's "So then" is functionally equivalent but a minority rendering. |

### 4:8a — Lexical
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "Draw near" |
| LOGOS | "Draw near" |
| PNEUMA | "Come close" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Draw near"** |
| Rationale | eggisate = draw near/approach. EMET/LOGOS agree on the more dignified English equivalent. |

### 4:8b — Lexical
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "Cleanse your hands" |
| LOGOS | "Cleanse your hands" |
| PNEUMA | "Wash your hands" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Cleanse your hands"** |
| Rationale | katharisate = cleanse/purify (ritual/moral connotation). "Cleanse" is more precise than "wash." |

### 4:8c — Phrasing (double-minded)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "double-minded ones" |
| LOGOS | "you double-minded" |
| PNEUMA | "you who are torn between loyalty to God and loyalty to the world" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"you double-minded"** |
| Rationale | Greek dipsuchoi = double-souled/double-minded. PNEUMA's expansion, while interpretively valid, departs from the source. |

### 4:9a — Lexical (first imperative)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "Be wretched" |
| LOGOS | "Lament" |
| PNEUMA | "Let there be tears for what you have done" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Lament"** |
| Rationale | talaiporesate: "lament" captures the imperative mourning sense. PNEUMA's paraphrase is too free. EMET's "be wretched" is awkward as a command. |

### 4:9b — Lexical
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "dejection" |
| LOGOS | "gloom" |
| PNEUMA | "gloom" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"gloom"** |
| Rationale | katepheian = downcast look, gloom. LOGOS/PNEUMA agree; EMET's "dejection" is acceptable but a minority choice. |

### 4:10 — Lexical / Addition
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "he will exalt you" |
| LOGOS | "he will exalt you" |
| PNEUMA | "he will lift you up in honor" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he will exalt you"** |
| Rationale | hupsosi = exalt/lift up. EMET/LOGOS agree. PNEUMA adds "in honor" — not in the Greek text. |

### 4:11a — Phrasing (address)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "brothers" |
| LOGOS | "brothers" |
| PNEUMA | "Brothers and sisters" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"brothers"** |
| Rationale | Greek adelphoi is grammatically masculine, used as an inclusive address. EMET/LOGOS preserve the formal equivalent. |

### 4:11b — Paraphrase (PNEUMA)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "speaks against the law and judges the law" |
| LOGOS | "speaks against the law and judges the law" |
| PNEUMA | "criticizing and judging God's law... your job is to obey the law, not to judge whether it applies to you" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"speaks against the law and judges the law"** |
| Rationale | PNEUMA significantly paraphrases, adding interpretive material. EMET/LOGOS agree on a faithful rendering. |

### 4:12 — Capitalization
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "Lawgiver and Judge" (capitalized) |
| LOGOS | "lawgiver and judge" (lowercase) |
| PNEUMA | "the Judge" (paraphrased) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Lawgiver and Judge"** |
| Rationale | As divine titles referring to God, capitalization is warranted. EMET's convention is adopted. |

### 4:13 — Lexical
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "make profit" |
| LOGOS | "make a profit" |
| PNEUMA | "make money" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"make a profit"** |
| Rationale | kerdeso = gain/profit. EMET/LOGOS agree. PNEUMA's "make money" is a looser equivalent. |

### 4:14 — Lexical (mist/vapor)
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "vapor" |
| LOGOS | "mist" |
| PNEUMA | "morning mist" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"mist"** |
| Rationale | atmis = vapor/mist/steam. "Mist" is natural English. PNEUMA adds "morning" — not in the Greek. |

### 4:14b — Phrasing
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "for a little time appearing, and then disappearing" |
| LOGOS | "appears for a little time and then vanishes" |
| PNEUMA | "appears for a little while, then vanishes" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"appears for a little while and then vanishes"** |
| Rationale | Blend of LOGOS/PNEUMA. All three agree in substance. |

### 4:15 — Phrasing
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "If the Lord might will, and we will live, and we will do this or that" |
| LOGOS | "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that" |
| PNEUMA | "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that"** |
| Rationale | LOGOS/PNEUMA are identical. EMET's "might will" is awkward in English, though it reflects the Greek subjunctive. |

### 4:17 — Phrasing
| Translation | Rendering |
|-------------|-----------|
| EMET | "sin it is to him" |
| LOGOS | "to him it is sin" |
| PNEUMA | "for you it is sin" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to him it is sin"** |
| Rationale | EMET's word order is too literal. PNEUMA shifts from third person to second person (not in Greek). LOGOS preserves the Greek dative construction faithfully. |

---

## Pattern Analysis

### EMET tendencies in James 4:
- Overly wooden English word order (vv. 2, 3, 15, 17)
- Preserves grammatical ambiguity well (v. 5)
- Faithful to Sinaiticus readings but sometimes at the cost of clarity

### LOGOS tendencies in James 4:
- Consistently the closest to the consensus (chosen as primary basis in ~14 of 17 verses)
- Balances formal accuracy with readable English
- Occasionally under-capitalizes divine titles (v. 12)

### PNEUMA tendencies in James 4:
- Adds interpretive material not in the Greek (vv. 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14)
- Neutralizes metaphors for accessibility (v. 4 "adulteresses" to "unfaithful people")
- Shifts grammatical person for rhetorical effect (v. 17)
- Section headings and expanded paraphrases depart from source text

### Consensus leaned toward:
- **LOGOS:** 14 verses (primary basis)
- **EMET:** 1 element adopted over LOGOS (v. 12 capitalization)
- **PNEUMA:** 1 element adopted over others (v. 4 "chooses")
- **Blend:** 2 verses drew from multiple translations
